item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an interasting read
Upvoted 1+

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The first image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0), as the closest source I could find, here, has no indication that the image was released under Creative Commons. As such, the image has been removed. The other two images are cc-compliant, as the second one comes from here (as mentioned by anqxyr in this post), which is licensed under CC BY 2.0, and the third image comes from here, also licensed under CC BY 2.0.

EDIT: The non-compliant image has been replaced with a cc-compliant alternative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Each figurine depicts a human form (or, in some cases, an animal form), typically depicted in a threatening posture.

Since there are only 7 of these things, is it correct to say that each of them depicts a human if there are some that don't? I'd just say "each figurine depicts a human or animal form."

Also, is figurine (and later, statuette) the correct word? These things look big.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand that, I just brought it up because I have seen differentiation done on other SCP's, such as through the use of colour to indicate speaker. Just a thought, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The statue doesn't literally speak: the incorporeal entity bound to the statue speaks through the witch doctor's mouth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since the Audio Logs with SCP-1036-6 are transcribed, I'd much rather there be some differentiation between when SCP-1036-6 and Nzinga are speaking, with a note to say that all the words are coming from Nzinga's mouth, rather than an unwieldy block of text. Would make reading that section much easier, and in an actual scientific report, would probably be done for the exact same reason.
I liked this SCP, but I felt like it was missing a little something. Can't put my finger on it though.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I see what you're trying to do, but the description of SCP-158 explicitly says that it only works on organisms.

I completely missed that. Damn. Devil is in the details.

Will keep an eye out for more suitable crosslink targets. This one in particular is difficult as it hits that shamanistic magic vibe in just such a way there is little similar to it on the site that I found in my digging.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I see what you're trying to do, but the description of SCP-158 explicitly says that it only works on organisms.

I could see a cross-test of SCP-158 and SCP-1036 being a fucking fine tale, though.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer removed. No edits made per author request.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, that is a fun SCP. While reading, I was wondering if perhaps one could use SCP-158 to extract the captured spirits within the the 1036 instances, and transfer them to a more secure vessel to prevent the potential release of what appear to be very angry spirits. Then I thought, that the on site shamans would probably protest to that notion, and such a transfer could itself result in a containment breach, and so such a proposal may get shot down for the current protocol that has been shown to work. Recommending the following edits as a result:

Changing this:

Each specimen is associated with a sentient incorporeal entity that had become bound to its respective specimen, prior to the Foundation's acquisition of the specimen in question, by means of a traditional Kongo shamanistic ritual practice. Each of the specimens in the Foundation's custody were constructed (inclusive of the performance of the binding protocol) no more recently than the mid-1870s.1 So long as the integrity of its respective SCP-1036 specimen is maintained, the entity is incapable of free agency apart from communicating with a nganga or interacting with a living creature that makes eye contact with the specimen. Specimens of SCP-1036 were constructed by indigenous nganga in order to apply the powers of the bound entity for various useful purposes, but knowedge of the protocol for doing so has been lost; accordingly the Foundation's efforts regarding SCP-1036 have focused on containment.

To this:

Each specimen is associated with a sentient incorporeal entity that had become bound to its respective specimen, prior to the Foundation's acquisition of the specimen in question, by means of a traditional Kongo shamanistic ritual practice. Each of the specimens in the Foundation's custody were constructed (inclusive of the performance of the binding protocol) no more recently than the mid-1870s.1 So long as the integrity of its respective SCP-1036 specimen is maintained, the entity is incapable of free agency apart from communicating with a nganga or interacting with a living creature that makes eye contact with the specimen. Specimens of SCP-1036 were constructed by indigenous nganga in order to apply the powers of the bound entity for various useful purposes, but knowedge of the protocol for doing so has been lost; accordingly the Foundation's efforts regarding SCP-1036 have focused on containment. Proposals to use SCP-158 or other similar devices to move captured entities to more secured containment vessels have been rejected following protest from on-site nganga, and the potential for entity release during such a transfer.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I think this is a little too much for an Euclid:

It is not known whether any indigenous nganga, other than those employed by the Foundation, are still extant in central Africa and thus capable of performing the binding ceremony to create additional instances of SCP-1036. In order to reduce the likelihood of this, Foundation agents have encouraged the deforestation of ██████ and the removal of indigenous traditional societies from that area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for providing the Scientific American snippet. I see now that it makes sense in universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never says they actually went through with it; only that some have suggested it, and considering that this one in-universe dates back to at least 1902, that's actually very plausible.

The science magazine Scientific American does a monthly feature where they feature article excerpts from that month's issues 50, 100, and 150 years prior. One such snippet a while back was an editorial from 1856 criticizing the following suggestion in a paper presented to the British Association for the Advancement of Science by one Señor Susini of Spain:

The regions watered by the Amazonas, reclaimed from the savage tribes, ferocious animals, and noxious reptiles which now infest them, and traversed by the plowshare might be capable of sustaining the entire globe.

To SA's credit, they essentially called this an idiotic plan, a desecration of a natural wonder, and an insult to the indigenous peoples of South America (bonus points: they actually used the term "the indigenous peoples".) Moral of the story: people were both actually that stupid and actually that enlightened back then, just like now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the necropost, but I take issue with the fact that the Foundation would encourage rainforest deforestation and removal of indigenous societies as a side-procedure. It's unrealistic and presumably a waste of resources. If no nganga are present, why bother destroying both a culture and an area? It seems to overstep the 'cold, not cruel' policy demonstrated in other articles. On top of all that, the entire footnote adds nothing to the original article or build upon the central concept. Do I have permission to edit?

+1 otherwise.

item.title: You stole my soul!
item.content:encoded: Thanks for writing my favorite skip and stealing my sleep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a small thing that's bugging me: there are six or seven sentences beginning with "each specimen/figurine" in the description, in very close proximity to each other.

Still upvoting because it's a great article. Lots of articles have a "people go crazy and hurt themselves" section, and it often falls flat for lack of originality or descriptive power, but this one actually made me cringe a little. Conversations with entities are also hard to do right, but even though it was just a little bit hard to follow because of the wall-o-text effect, I think the struggle between the nganga and the pissed-off, uncooperative entity was portrayed really well. I just thought if the author was still around, fixing that "each" issue would take the article from a 9.5 to a 10.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very enjoyable, creative and specific expletives are always fun. Slightly unnerving to me specifically because my name is Foy and gasoline isn't very nice to stomachs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent work.

As someone with a background in anthropology, I really must commend you for your careful research and incorporation of real-life African culture into this.

Upvoted.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The second image in this article is a CC-BY-2.0 image by Jean-Pierre Dalbéra via Wikimedia Commons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"If anything, isn't creating these relics restraining anomalous entities of potentially dangerous nature, rather than letting them run free? Why then would the Foundation seek to destroy it?"

As far as we can tell, the entities aren't dangerous until they've become angered by being bottled up. By analogy, separate piles of sulfur and saltpeter aren't dangerous; it's only when they're combined that they become dangerous. And in my headcanon, the Foundation is a big fan of brute force solutions: if it's possible to stop a dangerous anomalous practice by neutralizing everyone who knows how to accomplish that practice, then the neutralization may be worthwhile. Compare the extermination of the whale species in SCP-1012.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the Foundations hasn't seen whatever entity these are imprisoning, and therefore don't care. If there is a definite thing being stopped, the foundation will help stop it (see SCP-231), but otherwise it does not matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something I don't understand about this one.

The creation of these objects is an indigenous religious rite, presumably as part of a greater faith rather than a single act of reality-violation. If anything, isn't creating these relics restraining anomalous entities of potentially dangerous nature, rather than letting them run free? Why then would the Foundation seek to destroy it - do they take similar precautions against any and all Abrahamic religious practices that may be efficacious in-setting? Or is this just a case of a particularly hideously racist attitude justifying it?

For that matter, if they're encouraging the destruction of an indigenous faith, how are they getting the nganga employed with them to cooperate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't know how much I want to upvote "one who fornicates with goats" :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the show Oddities, they had this freaky cauldron thing with rusted chains and knives and nails and stuff all over it, and they had to X-ray it to try and see what was inside. They referred to it as an nganga. I thought it was the most awesome thing ever, and I wanted to own it. I'm glad there's an SCP that evokes that same feeling, because I loved that thing. Really awesome SCP regardless, it just reminded me of that cauldron thing. >__>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic. Absolutely incredible.

Curiosity: is this based on a real African culture's tradition/practice? If not, it's still really well thought out, but if so, a good amount of research must have been put in.

I'd +2 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your first point makes me like it more, actually. Maybe they did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (expletive, translates to "Upvoted. Fucking creepy, man.")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was all "it's a thing what makes you crazy." But then I got to the second picture and Jesus fuck shit buggery. I've never liked fetishes. The inventive deaths in the containment breach, the Foundation having a witch doctor, the fact that they're contained but sort of only barely, and the beautiful final footnote have made this one of the best SCPs out there.

One detriment, in my opinion: while the some of the translated insults are brilliant and create the perfect tone (diseased penis; place a leech in your anus) the ones that are translated as actual profanity spoil the tone a little, and would work as effectively as tribally insults while staying detached if they used non-profane language. Suggested: "one who fornicates with goats" and "hippopotamus faeces".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man I wish I had found this sooner because it is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With nail fetishes IRL, it's actually the reverse of what's in the procedures. The nails are put in to release the power of the spirit. At least, that's how I understand it. In-universe, it's probably like what Bland said.

Also, I almost upvoted this based solely on the picture. After three sentences, I upvoted based on the Foundation having a staff witch doctor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was this, by any chance, inspired by a certain Richard Matheson story?

I liked it, particularly the aspect of the Foundation having to work with African witch doctors to understand and contain an SCP. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very well done on a whole lot of levels. The last footnote in particular - more than just showing how the Foundation can be extremely amoral to the point of being arguably immoral, it also feels very believable: I venture to suggest that it was much easier for the agents to make this call when it was a bunch of African indigenous tribes-people involved. If a similar circumstance happened in European/American/Canadian/Australian/etc suburbia, I sincerely doubt that the Foundation would have tried forcibly relocating & razing a massive area on that kind of pretext. (Note that I'm sure the Foundation would do that if the threat were greater.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, they are good.
See, it adds to authenticity. It's .. things english lacks a word for, much like there's no word for motherfucker in slovak, and the verb "skurvit sa" (to become like a whore would be closest translation) i would have trouble finding the english equivalent of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just a pet peeve of mine. Sort of like when comedians say that they wish they were gynecologists. Trust me, you don't. I've done enough paps and delivered enough kids to know that it isn't fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this would be the fastest upvote I've made so far here, but I couldn't stop reading it.

Everything works here. From the spirit's dialogue giving it a much more interesting character than most ghostly things I've seen, to the creepiness of the actual incident, to the horrible sociopathy inherent in the last footnote. I especially liked the way that I can actually see why this thing would be so angry and murderous after having been trapped there for so long.

However, I agree that the transcript of the spirit's communications could be improved by removing the excess translation notes. Given the whole monologue is in Kikongo, we don't need to be told the expletives are being translated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cold iron is in a lot of stories for stopping spirit stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Studying this kind of thing, I doubt it's an M.D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, they didn't MEAN to kill them. They're just the ones that carried the spirits of the three statues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, what's the point of the nails? I get that it's supposed to be part of the binding ritual (or maintaining the binding spell), but why?

(And the possessed D-Class putting the nails into himself… Punishment by the entity, or something else?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is always room left for more nails…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens when there's no room left for more nails?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few nitpicks:

  1. "your years" isn't needed, unless you mean to imply that the spirits come from a different planet.
  2. I don't think that you need so many translation notes. Just put in "goat fucker", "hippopotamus shit eater", etc. Probably the only one that actually needs it is the one that translates to "medicine".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck. Yes. This is brilliant in its horrific nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I chalk that up to "using the vernacular." It's the same way an IT tech will tell you to click the browser, not "activate the program's alias": yes there's technical/accurate terms, but sometimes you need to put it in a way that the folks around understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read it the first time, didn't see the footnote, upvoted.

Just re-read it, saw the footnote, considered making a second account to double upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. I especially like the uncertainty in the last sentence of the second interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instant classic. Spooked my guts off. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love everything. The concept, the images (Especially how it goes from the tamer first image to the JEEBUSWTFery of the second statue's face), and the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concept is (to me) un-great, execution is fucking fantastic. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderful read. One little possible nitpick, if that's ok. I'm not sure what type of doctor Morrison is supposed to be, but a medical doctor would know that you can't swallow your tongue. You can choke on it if it swells, but the way it is attached to the posterior jaw/anterior pharynx makes it impossible to swallow. Again, a small nitpick-feel free to ignore me.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit, my first thought was 'oh great, another soul-trap'. Then I kept reading. Then I read 'one who eats hippopotamus shit'.

Then I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude got lucky. It's a thing that happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It said that everyone else at the Site died except for him, and I don't think that three batshit crazy people could wipe out an entire base filled with people that have guns. I think the implication was that everyone but him went crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Three broken statues, three possessed people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a little confused there as well. Maybe a few months later he'll die of a rare African parasite or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did that last survivor survive?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article's well-written. The concept is pretty good. I like the characterization of the bound entity, especiallyits anger and profanity.

Curiously, though, the bit that earned my instant upvote was that footnote. Sweet jesus, the Foundation are cold-blooded bastards — and yes, they have to be, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent, Spike. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking fantastic. This is an exemplary article. Well done.
